epigraph.text: The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.

content.text:
Every logical system must begin somewhere. In algebra, we assume the 
existence of numbers and the rules of arithmetic. In geometry, we begin 
with three **undefined terms**: point, line, and plane. These terms 
are so fundamental that we cannot define them using simpler concepts. 
Instead, we describe their properties and relationships.

content.title: Undefined Terms
content.content:
The following terms are accepted without formal definition:

- A **point** is a location in space. It has no 
 size, no length, width, or height. We represent points with dots and 
 name them with capital letters: $A$, $B$, $P$.

- A **line** is a straight path extending infinitely 
 in both directions. It has no thickness and no endpoints. We denote a 
 line through points $A$ and $B$ as $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$.

- A **plane** is a flat surface extending 
 infinitely in all directions. It has no thickness. We name planes with 
 capital script letters ($\mathcal{P}$, $\mathcal{Q}$) or by three 
 noncollinear points (plane $ABC$).

content.text:
Although we cannot define these terms, we can visualize them. A point is 
like an infinitely small dot. A line is like an infinitely long, perfectly 
straight wire with no thickness. A plane is like an infinitely large, 
perfectly flat sheet of paper.

content.title: Collinear Points
content.content:
Points that lie on the same line are called **collinear**.
Points that do not lie on the same line are **noncollinear**.

content.title: Coplanar Points
content.content:
Points that lie on the same plane are called **coplanar**.
Points that do not lie on the same plane are **noncoplanar**.

content.title: Identifying collinear points
content.solution:
Points $A$, $B$, and $C$ are collinear because they all lie on the same line.
Point $D$ is not on this line, so $D$ is not collinear with $A$, $B$, and $C$ 
together. However, any two points are always collinear (since a unique line 
passes through any two points), so $A$ and $D$ are collinear, $B$ and $D$ 
are collinear, etc.

content.text:
Just as undefined terms give us objects to work with, postulates give 
us rules about how these objects behave. The following postulates 
establish the basic relationships among points, lines, and planes.

content.title: The Point-Line Postulate
content.content: Through any two distinct points, there exists exactly one line.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This means two things: (1) a line through those points *exists*, 
and (2) it is *unique*—there is only one such line.

content.title: The Line-Plane Postulate
content.content:
If two points lie in a plane, then the entire line containing them lies 
in that plane.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: The Three-Point Postulate
content.content: Through any three noncollinear points, there exists exactly one plane.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This is why a three-legged stool never wobbles—three points determine 
a unique plane!

content.title: The Intersection Postulates
content.content:
1. If two distinct lines intersect, they intersect in exactly 
 one point.

2. If two distinct planes intersect, they intersect in exactly 
 one line.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Applying the postulates
content.problem:
Use the postulates to answer each question.

1. How many lines pass through both points $P$ and $Q$?

2. Three noncollinear points $A$, $B$, $C$ are given. How many 
 planes contain all three?

3. Two planes intersect. What describes their intersection?
content.solution:
1. By the Point-Line Postulate, exactly one line passes through 
 any two distinct points.

2. By the Three-Point Postulate, exactly one plane contains 
 three noncollinear points.

3. By the Intersection Postulate, two distinct planes intersect 
 in exactly one line.

content.title: Space
content.content:
**Space** is the set of all points. It extends infinitely 
in all directions and contains all possible geometric figures.

content.text:
In this course, we work primarily in a single plane (plane geometry), but 
we will also encounter three-dimensional figures (solid geometry) when we 
study surface area and volume.

marginNotes.text:
Who has measured the waters in the hollow 
of his hand and marked off the heavens with a span?

When he established the heavens, I was 
there; when he drew a circle on the face of the deep.
